title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read an article from which six sentences have been removed. Choose from the sentences A–G the one which fits each gap (37–42). There is one extra sentence which you do not need to use.

content.title: Pages for Everyone: Community Libraries and Literacy
content.text: On a street corner in a quiet suburb of Portland, Oregon, a small wooden box on a post holds a dozen paperbacks and a handwritten sign that reads: 'Take a book, leave a book.' It is a Little Free Library — one of more than 150,000 registered across ninety countries. (37) [37] Este pequeño gesto de generosidad refleja un movimiento global que lleva libros a quienes más los necesitan. [/37] What began as one man's tribute to his late mother has become a worldwide network dedicated to making reading accessible to all. || The relationship between access to books and literacy rates is well established. Children who grow up in homes without books are statistically less likely to become confident readers, regardless of how good their schools are. (38) [38] Las bibliotecas comunitarias intentan romper este ciclo llevando libros directamente a los barrios. [/38] In food deserts — areas with limited access to fresh produce — the equivalent 'book desert' is just as damaging to long-term educational outcomes. || Larger community libraries take many forms. In countries across Africa and South Asia, bicycle libraries bring collections to remote villages where no fixed library exists. (39) [39] Estos proyectos móviles demuestran que las barreras geográficas no tienen por qué ser un obstáculo para la lectura. [/39] In Bangladesh, boat libraries navigate river communities that are cut off from urban services for months at a time during the monsoon season. || Beyond literacy, community libraries serve as social infrastructure. In many towns, especially those that have lost post offices, banks and local shops, the library is the last public space where people from all walks of life naturally gather. (40) [40] Este papel social convierte a la biblioteca en algo más que un depósito de libros. [/40] Librarians often serve informally as advisers, advocates and even counsellors for community members who have nowhere else to turn. || Digital technology has presented both a challenge and an opportunity. E-books and digital lending platforms have expanded access enormously, particularly for people with visual impairments or those living in areas without physical libraries. (41) [41] Sin embargo, la brecha digital deja atrás a quienes no tienen dispositivos ni conexión a internet. [/41] Community libraries that offer both physical books and digital access points are better positioned to serve the full diversity of their communities. || Ultimately, the case for community libraries rests on a simple premise. (42) [42] Leer es una habilidad fundamental, y el acceso a los libros no debería depender del código postal o del nivel de ingresos. [/42] Every initiative that brings books closer to people who might not otherwise encounter them is an investment in individual potential and social cohesion.

paragraphs.A: Volunteers who staff community libraries often develop deep personal connections with regular visitors.
paragraphs.B: This social role transforms the library into something far more than a repository of books.
paragraphs.C: Reading is a fundamental skill, and access to books should not depend on postcode or income.
paragraphs.D: These mobile projects prove that geography need not be a barrier to literacy.
paragraphs.E: Yet the digital divide means that those without devices or connectivity risk being left further behind.
paragraphs.F: This small act of generosity represents a worldwide movement to bring books to those who need them most.
paragraphs.G: Community libraries try to break this cycle by bringing books directly into neighbourhoods.

questions.explanation: F connects the concrete example of the small library with the broader global movement described in the following sentence.
G introduces the function of community libraries as a solution to the access problem, preparing the analogy with 'book deserts'.
D asserts that mobile projects overcome geographical barriers, connecting the examples of the bicycle and the boat.
B elevates the social value of the library beyond books, linking to the informal role of librarians as advisers.
E introduces the counterpoint of the digital divide after mentioning the benefits of technology, with the implicit adversative conjunction.
C presents the fundamental premise of the article — equal access to reading — closing the text with a statement of principles.
